coredata.dc:title: Multi-Purpose electrochemical tyrosinase nanobiosensor based on poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots: Applications to hormone drugs analyses and inhibition studies
coredata.dc:creator: Erkmen, Cem
Demir, Yeliz
Kurbanoglu, Sevinc
Uslu, Bengi
coredata.dc:description: In this work, a novel multi-purpose amperometric nanobiosensor based on tyrosinase enzyme for the determination of phenolic compounds, catechol, epinephrine, and norepinephrine, was developed. This novel nanobiosensor was prepared from Tyrosinase enzyme immobilization in poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots platform via crosslinking with glutaraldehyde. Parameters that can affect biosensing, such as graphene quantum dots, poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles, tyrosinase quantities, pH, and temperature, were optimized. Under optimized conditions, determination of phenolic compounds was achieved in the range 0.005−11 μM for catechol, 0.2−12 μM for epinephrine, and 0.1–2.5 μM for norepinephrine. Limit of detection values were found as 0.002 μM, 0.065 μM, 0.035 μM for catechol, epinephrine, and norepinephrine, respectively, with acceptable repeatability and reproducibility results. Moreover, the developed nanobiosensor was further successfully utilized to follow inhibition of tyrosinase by a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, diclofenac, with different incubation times and concentration levels. Hence, I50 was reported as 4.87 mM diclofenac. The optimized biosensor was also applied for the detection of epinephrine and norepinephrine in pharmaceutical dosage forms, catechol from human serum.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Cem Erkmen is currently a PhD student in Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, in Turkey. He graduated from Chemical Engineering Department in 2014. He completed his master's degree about HILIC chromatography in 2017. He has been a researcher of the PhD Scholarship 100/2000 YÖK since 01.01.2018, sponsored by the Council of Higher Education (YÖK). He has also been a scholar of the 2211/A National PhD Scholarship Program since 09.08.2019, sponsored by The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK). He has authored or co-authored more than 17 peer-reviewed full papers and more than 6 book chapters. His areas of interest are electrochemistry, biosensor, nanosensor, aptasensor, drug analysis, redox mechanisms of drug active compounds, spectrophotometry, validation, separation techniques, biological sample analysis, and modified electrodes. He has presented poster or oral presentation in national and international congresses and has ongoing studies. He received in 2018, ‘The Best Oral Presentation Award’ in 4 th International Conference on Analytical Chemistry (RO-ICAC’2018).

Bengi Uslu is currently a full Professor in Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, in Turkey. Her areas of interest are electrochemistry, biosensors, nanosensors, biotechnology, separation techniques, liquid chromatography, drug analysis, bioanalytical method development, redox mechanisms of drugs, spectrophotometry and analytical method validation. Her postdoctoral research, sponsored by The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey, was conducted with Prof. Emil Palecek at Institue of Biophysics, Lab of Biophysical Chemistry and Molecular Oncology in Czech Republic. She has authored or co-authored more than 170 peer-reviewed full papers and more than 170 oral or poster presentations in scientific conferences. She has more than 30 book chapters and took part in the translation of many books into Turkish. She has more than 3300 citations of research papers with an h-index of 33. Prof. Dr. Bengi Uslu is currently the editor of book 'Electroanalytical Applications of Quantum Dot Based Biosensors' which is which is published in Elsevier in 2021. She has completed 16 national/international projects and has ongoing 5 projects. She is an active member of European Directorate for the Quality of Medicines (EDQM) 10A and The International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (ICHQ14). She received Ankara University Scientific Support Award in 2008, Ankara University Scientific Award in 2014 and Academy of Pharmacy Science Award of Turkish Pharmaceutical Association in 2015.

head.ce:title: Multi-Purpose electrochemical tyrosinase nanobiosensor based on poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots: Applications to hormone drugs analyses and inhibition studies

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, 06560, Ankara, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ankara University, Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, 06560, Ankara/TURKEY

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a novel multi-purpose amperometric nanobiosensor based on tyrosinase enzyme for the determination of phenolic compounds, catechol, epinephrine, and norepinephrine, was developed. This novel nanobiosensor was prepared from Tyrosinase enzyme immobilization in poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles decorated graphene quantum dots platform via crosslinking with glutaraldehyde. Parameters that can affect biosensing, such as graphene quantum dots, poly (3,4 ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles, tyrosinase quantities, pH, and temperature, were optimized. Under optimized conditions, determination of phenolic compounds was achieved in the range 0.005−11 μM for catechol, 0.2−12 μM for epinephrine, and 0.1–2.5 μM for norepinephrine. Limit of detection values were found as 0.002 μM, 0.065 μM, 0.035 μM for catechol, epinephrine, and norepinephrine, respectively, with acceptable repeatability and reproducibility results. Moreover, the developed nanobiosensor was further successfully utilized to follow inhibition of tyrosinase by a non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug, diclofenac, with different incubation times and concentration levels. Hence, I50 was reported as 4.87 mM diclofenac. The optimized biosensor was also applied for the detection of epinephrine and norepinephrine in pharmaceutical dosage forms, catechol from human serum.

ce:section.ce:para: Conducting polymers have been attracted many interests of researchers from the beginning of the discovery of the first conducting polymer [1–5]. Besides their unique properties, such as high electrical conductivity and environmental stability, they have some problems such as solubility, moldability, and processability, which are the most fundamental and clearly defined problems. To overcome these problems, scientists have suggested several methods such as introducing side chains, doping with inorganic materials, creating colloids, etc. Synthesizing polymeric nanoparticles based on conducting polymers is also another way of solving these problems [6,7]. Subsequently, a polymeric nanoparticle dispersed in water or alcoholic solvent can be prepared; hence, the polymerization media is generally inorganic solvent, which cannot be used to immobilize biological materials. Besides all conducting polymeric nanoparticles, poly (3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles (PEDOT NPs) have been used commonly due to their low bandgap, conductivity, stability, and doped transparency properties [8–11].

After the discovery of Quantum dots (QDs) in 1980s [12], they offer new platforms not only in construction of optical biosensors but also in gene delivery [13], drug delivery [14], bio-sensing [15], monitoring glucose [16], determination of pharmaceuticals [17,18], cellular imaging, such as HeLa cells [19], human lung cancer cell [20], bio-imaging [21] etc. Such wide spectrum applications of QDs make these unique materials as multi-purposed materials [22–26].

Tyrosinase (Tyr, EC 1.14.18.1), which catalyzes the biosynthesis of melanin and other pigments through oxidation from tyrosine, is a copper-containing metalloenzyme found in the plant bacterial, mammalian, fungal tissues [27,28]. Tyr causes hyperpigmentation and hypopigmentation in mammals, and it is vital to control enzyme activity with Tyr inhibitors [29]. Catecholamines, dopamine, catechol (CAT), norepinephrine (NEPI), and epinephrine (EPI) are well-known Tyr substrates synthesized from the amino acid tyrosine taken by diet in central and peripheral neurons and the adrenal medulla. Catecholamine-secreting cells convert tyrosine into l-DOPA and then dopamine in a series of several reactions. Depending on cell type and cell homeostasis, dopamine can also be converted to NEPI or even converted to EPI [30,31]. Besides, CAT is the most important substrate of Tyr. Tyr catalyzes two specific reactions: hydroxylation of monophenols to o-diphenols (monophenolase activity), and o-diphenols are oxidized to o-quinones (diphenolase activity) as shown in reactions 1 and 2 [32,33].
(1)

EPI is an important inhibitory neurotransmitter, which exhibits a key role in the message transfer of biological activities in vivo. It is produced by the nerve tissue (including the brain) and the adrenal gland, showing hormone properties. The regulation of blood pressure, heart rate, immune system, lipolysis, and glycogen metabolism generally depends on the excess level of EPI in the human body. Besides, deficiency of EPI causes Parkinson's disease [34–37]. Another important transmitter is NEPI. It regulates blood pressure, mood disorders, and emotional arousal. Moreover, under stressful conditions, heart rate increases, pupils and air passages in the lungs expand, and blood vessels narrow due to the concentration of NEPI [38–40]. EPI and NEPI are extensively used for the treatment of many illnesses. On the other hand, concentrations of catecholamine in plasma are usually helpful for diagnosing and evaluating therapeutic and pharmacodynamic effects for neurological, psychiatric, and cardiovascular disorders. Therefore, it is important to develop selective, simple, and sensitive analytical methods for the determination of EPI and NEPI in clinical and pharmaceutical samples [41–43].

Biosensing platforms decorated with different nanomaterials have often been suggested [44,45]. Conducting polymers can be coupled with QDs; thus, innovative approaches in various electrochemical applications from this combination can be obtained [46,47]. Especially enzyme-based electrochemical nanobiosensors using QDs have been a new era for the enzyme biosensors; hence very limited researches have been published up to now with laccase [48–52], acetylcholinesterase [53,54], and glucose oxidase [55–60]. Moreover, using Tyrosinase, only three types of research exist in the literature using QDs [61–63]. In the catalytic mechanism of Tyrosinase, since molecular oxygen is formed in both reactions (Eqs. 1 and 2), amperometric methods can be used to follow enzyme activity and inactivity.

Hence in this work, a novel amperometric nanobiosensor was prepared from PEDOT NPs decorated graphene quantum dots (GQDs@PEDOT NPs) for the determination of phenolic compounds; CAT, EPI, and NEPI. The developed nanobiosensor was characterized by several techniques such as scanning electrochemical microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM), cyclic voltammetry (CV), chronoamperometric (CA), and electrochemical impedance (EIS) experiments. Using each substrate, CAT, EPI, and NEPI, the amount of PEDOT NPs, GQDs, Tyr amount, temperature, and pH parameters were optimized. Using the designed biosensor, EPI and NEPI were detected from pharmaceutical dosage forms, and CAT was detected from serum samples. Furthermore, the developed biosensor was further used to follow inhibition studies of Tyr by anti-inflammatory drug diclofenac through the decrease in CAT response.

For all CA measurements, the PalmSens4 potentiostat/galvanostat analyzer (Utrecht, The Netherlands) with a software package PSTrace Version 5.8 was used. Impedance spectroscopy and cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed by Autolab302 potentiostat/galvanostat/frequency-response analyzer PGST30 (Eco-Chemie, The Netherlands) and controlled by Nova Version 2.1 for characterization of the developed nanobiosensor. The surface morphology of electrodes was analyzed by scanning electrochemical microscopy (SEM) images from a field emission scanning electron microscope ZEISS EVO 40 (Merlin, Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany). Electrode samples for SEM analyses were first covered with a gold solution using AMITECH K 550 × . Moreover, the surface morphology of electrodes was analyzed with an atomic force microscope (AFM) (Veeco MultiMode V) at room temperature conditions. The screen-printed electrodes (SPE) were purchased from Metrohm, DropSens (Turkey), consisted of three electrodes system: a 3 mm diameter carbon as a working electrode, Ag/AgCl as a reference electrode, and carbon as a counter electrode. pH meter Model 538 (WTW, Austria) equipped with a combined glass electrode was used for pH measurements.

EPI, NEPI, and their pharmaceutical dosage forms (Penepin 0.15 mg / 0.3 mL Auto-Injector and Cardenor 4 mg) were supplied from VEM İlaç San. ve Tic. A.Ş, (Istanbul, Turkey). Graphene Quantum Dots in aqueous solution (DRP- DRP-GQD: λem =495 nm; concentration = 15 mg/mL; diameter Ø =100 nm) were purchased from DropSens (Llanera, Spain). Poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) nanoparticles (dispersion in H2O, ≤ 500 nm), glutaraldehyde (GA, 25 %), potassium phosphate dibasic, potassium phosphate monobasic, potassium chloride, sulfuric acid, ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, H2O2, potassium hexacyanoferrate (II) trihydrate, potassium hexacyanoferrate (III), mushroom Tyrosinase (≥ 1000 unit/mg), human serum (human male AB plasma), catechol and diclofenac sodium were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). All chemicals are of analytical grade. All solutions were prepared with ultra-pure water from a Millipore-Milli-Q system (Merck KGaA, Darmstadt, Germany) and kept at 4 °C when they are not in use.

50 mM phosphate buffer solution (PBS) containing KCl was prepared from monobasic and dibasic potassium phosphates. GA solution was prepared from 25 % GA stock solution by diluting with ultra-pure water. Tyr enzyme solution (1 mg/50 μL) was prepared in 50 mM, pH 6.50, KCl-free PBS, and necessary dilutions were achieved with the same buffer.

The surface of SPEs was activated with 0.1 M H2SO4 for 120 s at 3 mA using the chronopotentiometric method before each electrode modification. For electrode modification, as a first step, GQDs nanomaterial suspension was dropped onto the working surface of the SPE and allowed to dry at room temperature. PEDOT NPs were dropped onto SPE/GQDs surface and allowed to dry at room temperature in the next step. In the final step, the Tyr enzyme was dropped on the surface of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs. Tyr was immediately immobilized by crosslinking the SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr surface by adding a 0.25 % GA crosslinking agent. The prepared nanobiosensors were kept at +4 °C in a refrigerator overnight.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine using SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor from pharmaceutical dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: CA determination of EPI and NEPI was performed as follows: the prepared SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor was placed in an analytical cell containing 10 mL PBS with KCl (50 mM pH 6.5). 1.0 mM of the related substrate was added after achieving steady-state current under stirring conditions within a working potential of −0.2 V at 300 rpm. In order to check the accuracy of the developed nanobiosensors for EPI and NEPI, a stock solution of EPI was prepared using 3 mL of Penepin® (containing 15 mg of EPI) Auto –Injector and stock solution of NEPI was prepared using 4 mL of Cardenor® (containing 4 mg of NEPI) and diluted to the desired concentration by PBS. The amount of EPI and NEPI in pharmaceutical dosage forms was then calculated by the corresponding regression equation of calibration curves. For the recovery studies, a known amount of EPI and NEPI solutions were added to pre-analyzed pharmaceutical dosage form solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the Tyr inhibition: Dual detection of catechol and diclofenac
ce:section.ce:para: CA determination of CAT was performed using the optimized SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor under stirring conditions within a working potential of −0.2 V at 300 rpm. 1.0 mM of the related substrate was added after achieving a steady-state current. Moreover, recovery tests were performed for CAT using human male AB plasma serum samples. Known amounts of CAT were added to the human serum plasma and diluted with PBS. The recovery results were investigated using obtained calibration equations and reported in terms of RSD%. In order to perform inhibition studies of Tyr, a stock solution of diclofenac was prepared in ultra-pure water and diluted with PBS for inhibition time and inhibition concentration studies. Afterward, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor was inserted into the eppendorfs containing diclofenac solutions and allowed diclofenac to inhibit immobilized Tyr. Moreover, for incubation time and concentration studies, developed nanobiosensor was kept at different times in diclofenac solutions containing different amounts of diclofenac. In the final step, CA measurements were carried using inhibited nanobiosensor for catechol. Tyr inhibition was followed by the decrease in catechol response, and relative inhibition of Tyr by diclofenac was calculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphological characterizations of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Investigation of surface morphology using scanning electron microscopy technique is one of the most fundamental steps to prepare an ideal biosensor. The morphology of SPE/GQDs, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs, and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr were obtained by SEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, after immobilization of the GQDs on the SPE, a smooth and uniform layer of GQDs film was observed. It is compatible with the literature [64,65], indicating that the GQDs have been highly coated on the SPE. In the second modification step, a new porous layer was formed with PEDOT NPs on the surface of SPE/GQDs, which can be clearly observed in Fig. 1B. Moreover, Fig. 1C presents a uniform morphology with a crystallinity cauliflower-like structure when SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs and Tyr were combined.

AFM measurement is an effective method to provide surface morphology. Besides SEM, AFM measurements were performed to check the homogeneity and the surface roughness of the electrode surfaces. Fig. 1(D–F) shows three-dimensional AFM images of SPE/GQDs, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs, and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr. Fig. 1D revealed a relatively smooth surface for GQDs modified electrode. In the second modification step, the SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs morphology is displayed in the AFM image (Fig. 1E), indicating that PEDOT NPs offer homogenous and rough coverage of the SPE surface. Finally, the AFM image of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr (Fig. 1F) revealed a much rough surface for much better interaction between enzyme and substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characterization of the SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the electrochemical properties of SPE/Tyr, SPE/GQDs/Tyr, and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensors were determined by cyclic voltammetric technique. Fig. 2 A depicts the comparative cyclic voltammograms of step-by-step modification of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor towards 1.0 mM CAT. Well-defined, clearly identified redox peaks were observed with a reduction potential at −0.20 V for the developed nanobiosensors in 50 mM, pH 6.5 PBS, a typical CAT response in PBS [66]. Due to the presence of an additional conductive GQDs layer on the electrode surface, the current response of CAT using SPE/GQDs/Tyr was relatively higher than SPE/Tyr. Moreover, since PEDOT NPs are highly conductive polymeric nanoparticles, they significantly facilitated the electron transfer rate and improved the current response of CAT compared to SPE/GQDs/Tyr.

Furthermore, electrocatalytic responses of the SPE/Tyr, SPE/GQDs/Tyr, and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensors towards 1 mM CAT were evaluated through chronoamperometric responses, which were conducted at −0.2 V, while stirring at 300 rpm. The effect of modification steps for chronoamperometric responses can easily be seen in Fig. 2B. Additionally, the effect of modifications for chronoamperometric responses of EPI and NEPI can be seen in the supplementary figures (Figure S1).

EIS is another powerful technique for providing information about the charge transport process of electrodes at the electrode-electrolyte interface. The semicircular portion of the impedance exhibits the kinetically controlled electron charge transfer resistance (Rct), and the linear region following that reveals the diffusion process at low frequencies [67]. Nyquist plots of SPE, SPE/QDs, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs, and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr were studied in 5 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− with 0.1 M KCl as redox probe by EIS in the frequency range of 0.1 Hz – 100 kHz and are represented in Fig. 3 A. In impedimetric characterization, the semicircle diameters of the Nyquist plot reflect the electron transfer resistance (Rct). It is clear from the diameter of the semicircle of different electrodes that the conductivity of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs is higher compared to SPE, which is evident due to the higher conductance of PEDOT NPs. As PEDOT NPs have increased the surface area and conductivity, the Rct value decreases. Moreover, when Tyr is added to the surface of the modified electrode, the charge transfer resistance of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr was found to be higher than the modified electrode. This indicated successful immobilization of Tyr onto the surface due to the biological materials hindering the electron transfer rate, confirming immobilization [67]. According to the Fig. 3A, Rct values were obtained as 1.51 × 105 Ω, 2.64 × 104 Ω, 1.13 × 104 Ω, 3.83 × 104 Ω for SPE, SPE/QDs, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr, respectively. PEDOT NPs and GQDs have increased the surface area, contributing to an increase in the conductivity and decrease in Rct values. SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr electrode can be considered as an optimum nanobiosensor for detection of CAT, EPI and NEPI compared to SPE, SPE/GQDs, SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs.

Moreover, cyclic voltammetric behavior of the SPE and SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs were also followed by the 5.0 mM [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− containing 0.10 M KCl. The modification increases the surface area, as can clearly be seen from the cyclic voltammograms (Fig. 3B). As a further improvement, the effective surface area of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs was calculated from Randles–Sevcik Eq. (3) where Ip is peak current (μA), n refers to the electrons involved in the redox reaction, A is the effective surface area of the electrode (cm2), υ is scan rate (V.s −1), D is the diffusion coefficient (cm2.s−1), C is the concentration of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (mol. cm−3) [68–70].
(3) Ip = (2.69 × 105) n 3/2 A D1/2 ν 1/2 C0
From this equation, the effective surface area was calculated as 0.124 cm2 which is 1.77 times higher than the one for the bare SPE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the biosensing response and analytical characterization of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: After investigating the surface morphology for the designed nanobiosensor, the sensitive and selective CA method for detection of 1 mM CAT, EPI and NEPI was developed. Prior to detection and inhibition studies, amounts of GQDs, PEDOT NPs, Tyr, pH, and measuring temperature were optimized. In this study, the amount of GQDs was chosen as the first parameter to be investigated since it is the first layer in the developed nanobiosensor. The surface of the SPEs was modified between 1 μL and 5 μL of GQDs in the presence of constant amounts of PEDOT NPs and Tyr. Maximum responses towards CAT, EPI, and NEPI were obtained when using 2 μL GQDs. Especially at higher GQDs amounts for EPI and NEPI, irrevocable results were obtained due to the increased layer coverage of GQDs that resulted in decreasing electron transfer rate. In the second modification step, PEDOT NPs amounts varying between 0.5 μL and 5 μL were dropped onto 2 μL of GQDs. As maximum obtained responses were obtained using 1 μL of PEDOT NPs amount, it was chosen as the optimum amount for multi-purpose usage. When higher PEDOT NPs amount was immobilized on SPE/GQDs, electrocatalytic performance towards the CAT, EPI, and NEPI was decreased.

Moreover, in the last and important step, different amounts of Tyr between 0.5 μL and 7 μL were immobilized on SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs. When a higher Tyr amount was immobilized in GQDs@PEDOT NPs, nanocomposite matrix mislinkage has occurred since a high amount of Tyr could not crosslink with the same amount of glutaraldehyde. Therefore, the amount of 5 μL Tyr was found to be the optimum amount for CAT, EPI, and NEPI.

Additionally, after optimization of nanomaterial and enzyme amounts, experiments were carried out for operating temperature and pH. The nanobiosensor response was evaluated at a different operating temperature from 15 to 35 °C. The nanobiosensor response was increased as the temperature was increased up to 25 °C thereafter; it was decreased for CAT, EPI, and NEPI. Finally, the effect of the pH of the operating buffer on the nanobiosensor response was investigated. The pH was varied from 6.0–7.5. The nanobiosensor response was increased as the pH was increased up to pH 6.5, after which it starts decreasing for CAT, EPI, and NEPI. Detailed optimization results are presented in the supplementary file as figures S2, S3, and S4.

The selectivity of the developed nanobiosensor was verified by studying possible interferons and their potential impact on the analytical signal obtained. Na+, Cl−, Mg+2, Ca+2, ascorbic acid, uric acid, glucose, and H2O2 and a common excipient in these pharmaceuticals, Na2S2O5, were added to the buffer as 1 mM with 1:1 to the substrate ratio before the CAT, EPI, and NEPI addition. The lack of any change in the signals showed that the developed nanobiosensor is selective for CAT, EPI, and NEPI determination in the presence of these ions (Figure S5).

Additionally, the operational stability and shelf life of the developed nanobiosensor were investigated. For each compound, the prepared nanobiosensors were placed at 4 °C and evaluated daily for shelf-life studies. The current signal related to CAT, EPI, and NEPI found still retain approximately 90.0 % of its initial response after 1 day (Fig. 4 A). When stability results were realized, it can be said that developed nanobiosensor can be used as disposable. For the operational stability studies, developed nanobiosensors were checked with successive CAT measurements, EPI, and NEPI, individually (Fig. 4B). The developed nanobiosensor retained approximately 90 % of the original activity even after the first measurement. Hence, it can be said that the nanobiosensor exhibits long-term stability daily for CAT, EPI, and NEPI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine
ce:section.ce:para: For the determination of hormones, chronoamperometric responses of SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor were followed by continuous addition of EPI (Fig. 5 A) and NEPI (Fig. 5B) with different and increased concentrations under optimized conditions. Linear ranges were achieved in the range 0.2 − 12 μM for EPI and 0.1 − 2.5 μM for NEPI. Limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) based on 3 s/m and 10 s/m principles, respectively, were calculated using linear curves; where ‘s’ is the standard deviation of the peak currents of the lowest concentration of the analyte and ‘m’ is the slope of the related calibration curves. While LOD values were found as 0.065 μM, and 0.035 μM for EPI, and NEPI respectively, using SPE/GQD@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor, LOQ values are the lowest concentrations of the calibration curves.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of hormones epinephrine and norepinephrine using SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor from pharmaceutical dosage forms
ce:section.ce:para: In order to further show the applicability of the developed nanobiosensor in pharmaceutical dosage form analysis, commercial samples containing EPI and NEPI were analyzed. The accuracy of the developed methods was investigated by recovery tests after the addition of known amounts of pure drug to the pre-analyzed formulations of EPI and NEPI to determine whether excipients in the dosage form interfere with the analysis. Acceptable results in terms of average recovered %, RSD% of recovery, Bias% were obtained and reported.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition studies: dual detection and determination of catechol and diclofenac
ce:section.ce:para: Dual detection and determination of catechol and diclofenac were further investigated using SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor. Continuous addition of CAT (Fig. 5C), with different and increased concentrations under optimized conditions and linear relation was obtained between 0.005 and 11 μM CAT with LOD value as 0.002 μM, and LOQ value as 0.001 μM. For this study, detailed validation parameters are presented for multi-detection of CAT, EPI, and NEPI using SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor in Table 1 .

Additionally, CAT detection from spiked human serum was achieved by recovery tests from 2 μM human serum addition level. Acceptable results in terms of average recovered %, RSD% of recovery, Bias% were obtained and reported. The results show the validity of the proposed nanobiosensor for the quantitative determination of CAT, EPI, and NEPI, as shown in Table 2 .

In literature, it is stated that non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs are known to possess inhibitory potential against Tyr activity [71,72]. Moreover, it is reported that diclofenac also inhibits melanogenesis [73]. Hence, diclofenac showed inhibition through monophenolase or diphenolase activity of Tyr followed by spectrophotometric method [71]. There is no report on the electrochemical investigation of Tyrosinase inhibition by diclofenac. Therefore, the developed SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor was used to investigate Tyr inhibition by diclofenac through the decrease in CAT response.

Inhibition % is calculated as:
I n h i b i t i o n

I o

100
where Io current corresponds to the enzyme activity of the nanobiosensor when the inhibitor diclofenac is not present. Lower steady state-currents (I) refer to the catechol response after inhibition with diclofenac. Firstly, the incubation time was optimized since inhibition needs time. For optimizing the incubation time, firstly, the CA response for 10 μM catechol is recorded. The same nanobiosensor is incubated in 5 mM diclofenac for different incubation times (1, 5, 10, 15, 30, and 60 min). The optimum incubation time was found as 15 min with an inhibition % of 51.6 ± 0.86 (Fig. 6 A). Fig. 6B shows the change of the inhibition % with diclofenac concentration. In the linear range of 0.5−10 mM diclofenac, the inhibition effect of diclofenac on Tyr was followed. Using this inhibition strategy, the inhibition effect of diclofenac can be observed with the LOD value of 0.2 mM.

After optimization of the inhibition conditions, inhibitory strength, which is expressed as the I50 value, which is the half-maximal inhibitory concentration where 50 % inhibition is followed at a specific substrate concentration, was calculated [74]. Hence, I50 was reported as 4.87 mM diclofenac.

The catalytic mechanism of the enzyme biosensor was followed by enzyme kinetics studies using the Michaelis Menten model of a single substrate reaction for each CAT, EPI, and NEPI substrates. Firstly, saturation curves for the enzymatic reaction of Tyr for the substrates CAT, EPI, and NEPI were plotted (Fig. 7 ), showing that the reaction rate for CAT is relatively high compared to EPI and NEPI.

Afterward, Lineweaver Burk Plot is plotted, which is obtained from the calibration curves by plotting 1/I (μA−1) vs. 1/[S] (mM−1) [75–78]. From this graph, Km and Imax values were calculated using the following Michaelis – Menten’s relations [75–78];
1 I
=
1
I m a x
+
K m
I m a x
[ S ]

Using the Lineweaver Burk plot for EPI detection, Imax and Km values were calculated as 25.5 μA and 13 μM, respectively. It is also compatible with the saturation curves. For NEPI detection, Imax and Km values were calculated as, 13.6 μA and 11 μM, respectively. A lower Imax value was obtained than EPI, indicating that the reaction rate towards EPI is higher. Hence, Km values are nearly the same; this implies that the Tyr affinity towards these two substrates is nearly identical. For CAT detection, very low Km was reported as 2.5 μM, which indicates that the designed nanobiosensor showed the highest affinity towards CAT, rather than EPI and NEPI. Imax was calculated as 25.9 μA, for catechol detection. When we compare the obtained values, the highest affinity was obtained for catechol using this nanobiosensor. EPI has a high Imax value; controversially, the Km value is relatively low, indicating that the reaction rate is high, but every enzyme-substrate complex cannot result in the formation of the product. After inhibition of Tyr, the Imax value decreased to 1.95 μA as a nature of inhibition, the velocity of the enzymatic reaction decreases. The Km value was increased to 33 mM, indicating that enzyme-substrate affinity decreased significantly due to the inhibition. Since the Imax decreased as in irreversible inhibition type enzyme kinetics, and Km increased as in competitive inhibition type enzyme kinetics, one can suggest the inhibition mechanism as a mixed-type characteristic of enzyme kinetics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the performances of the developed nanobiosensor and other reported studies
ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme-based biosensors have found a universal acceptance in the biosensing area due to their selectivity and sensitivity. These biosensors present simple utilization by available instrumentation. The analytical performance of the developed SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor was compared with the performances of reported other nanosensors and biosensors (Table 3 ). In the literature, only three researches have been conducted using QDs and Tyr. Rather et al. [61], Manan et al. [59], and Han et al. [63] constructed Tyr biosensors modified with QDs using CAT and hydroxylated polychlorobiphenyls as substrates. Hence, in our study, multi-purpose usage of developed SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor for substrates including CAT, EPI, and NEPI was suggested, unlike other studies. One can notice that polymeric nanoparticle usage combined with QDs provides more sensitive results than other matrices. When the literature studies are considered, there are many different methods for the determination of EPI; hence, there is only one study using Tyr based biosensor. Gopal et al. [79] were able to determine 0.51 μM CAT in serum samples using Tyr modification on the MWCNT/GCE surface. More sensitive results were obtained in serum samples using PEDOT NPs and GQD modification than MWCNT modification in our study. QDs were used as modification agents in the determination of many analytes [79–82], as Jahani et al. [82] demonstrated in the determination of NEPI using GQDs. Our study shows the importance of polymeric nanoparticles and enzymes since almost two times sensitive results were obtained compared to Jahani’s study. Similar results were also observed when GQDs modification was used in CAT determination [80,81]. Although a wide linear range could not be obtained when using polymeric nanoparticles with GQDs, more sensitive determinations for CAT were obtained in this study.

In summary, an amperometric electrochemical nanobiosensor based on Tyr, GQDs, and PEDOT NPs has been developed for the first time for multi-purpose usage. The fabricated SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr electrode presents advantages, including biocompatibility with the Tyr enzyme, high electron-transfer efficiency, and enhanced analytical signal for CAT, EPI, and NEPI. The proposed nanobiosensor offers a wide linear response range for hormone drugs. LODs of 0.002 μM, 0.065 μM, and 0.035 μM for CAT, EPI, and NEP, respectively, due to the modification of SPE surface with GQDs, PEDOT NPs, and Tyr. Furthermore, the optimized nanobiosensor exhibited high sensitivity and low detection limit, with good selectivity and stability, and enabled successful detection of EPI and NEPI in pharmaceutical dosage forms and CAT in human serum samples. Furthermore, the developed SPE/GQDs@PEDOT NPs/Tyr nanobiosensor was used to investigate Tyr inhibition by diclofenac through the decrease in CAT response. Hence, this is the first report on the electrochemical investigation of Tyrosinase inhibition by diclofenac. The developed biochemical strategy of this electrochemical nanobiosensor would provide excellent potential for analysis of other substrates of Tyr, immobilization of different enzymes, and inhibition studies for other drugs, pesticides, ions etc.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Bengi Uslu is currently a full Professor in Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, in Turkey. Her areas of interest are electrochemistry, biosensors, nanosensors, biotechnology, separation techniques, liquid chromatography, drug analysis, bioanalytical method development, redox mechanisms of drugs, spectrophotometry and analytical method validation. Her postdoctoral research, sponsored by The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey, was conducted with Prof. Emil Palecek at Institue of Biophysics, Lab of Biophysical Chemistry and Molecular Oncology in Czech Republic. She has authored or co-authored more than 170 peer-reviewed full papers and more than 170 oral or poster presentations in scientific conferences. She has more than 30 book chapters and took part in the translation of many books into Turkish. She has more than 3300 citations of research papers with an h-index of 33. Prof. Dr. Bengi Uslu is currently the editor of book 'Electroanalytical Applications of Quantum Dot Based Biosensors' which is which is published in Elsevier in 2021. She has completed 16 national/international projects and has ongoing 5 projects. She is an active member of European Directorate for the Quality of Medicines (EDQM) 10A and The International Council for Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Pharmaceuticals for Human Use (ICHQ14). She received Ankara University Scientific Support Award in 2008, Ankara University Scientific Award in 2014 and Academy of Pharmacy Science Award of Turkish Pharmaceutical Association in 2015.

Sevinc Kurbanoglu is currently Associate Professor in Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, in Turkey. She was graduated from Middle East Technical University, Ankara in 2008. She got her master degree from same university, with the topic of conduction polymer based electrochemical biosensors. She studied in Prof. Dr. Lo GORTON Laboratory at Lund University, Sweden, as visiting researcher, during master studies. She involved in a project related with biosensing applications of engineered pyranose 2-oxidases mediated via osmium polymers in Lo Gorton’s Research Group, Sweden. She received her PhD degree in Analytical Chemistry, from Pharmacy Faculty of Ankara University in 2016 under the supervisor of Prof. Sibel A. OZKAN and co supervisor of Prof. Dr. Arben Merkoçi. She was also visiting researcher during PhD, in Prof. Dr. Arben Merkoçi’s Laboratory at Barcelona Autonoma University, Catalan Nanoscience and Nanotechnology Institute. Related with her PhD thesis topic, she conducted researches about tyrosinase inhibition through drugs and pesticides. For postdoctoral studies now, she is in collaboration with Prof. Dr. Frieder W. Scheller about molecularly imprinted polymers for drug analyses from Department of Analytical Biochemistry at Potsdam University. She published over 60 manuscripts in peer-reviewed journals and 20 book chapters. She was awarded with young scientist best oral presentation award from American Chemical Society in 2014 and 2016. She was awarded with Outstanding Success Award from Presidency of Higher Education Council of Turkey, as best PhD Thesis in 2017. Her research interests include biological and chemical nanosensors, enzyme based nanosensors, DNA based nanosensors, electrochemical assays for pharmaceutical compounds, validation, and electrochemical sensors.

Yeliz Demir received BSc degree in 2013 from Cumhuriyet University, Faculty of Science, Department of Chemistry and she received her MSc degree from the Medical Biochemistry, Department of the Medical School. In her master's degree, she worked on determining enzyme levels in the polyamine synthesis pathway in heart failure patients. She started her master's degree in Analytical Chemistry at the Faculty of Pharmacy at Ankara University in 2021 and is still continuing. Her areas of interest are electrochemistry, enzyme activity, analysis of drugs and biological samples, and nanosensor and biosensor studies.

Cem Erkmen is currently a PhD student in Ankara University Faculty of Pharmacy, Department of Analytical Chemistry, in Turkey. He graduated from Chemical Engineering Department in 2014. He completed his master's degree about HILIC chromatography in 2017. He has been a researcher of the PhD Scholarship 100/2000 YÖK since 01.01.2018, sponsored by the Council of Higher Education (YÖK). He has also been a scholar of the 2211/A National PhD Scholarship Program since 09.08.2019, sponsored by The Scientific and Technological Research Council of Turkey (TUBITAK). He has authored or co-authored more than 17 peer-reviewed full papers and more than 6 book chapters. His areas of interest are electrochemistry, biosensor, nanosensor, aptasensor, drug analysis, redox mechanisms of drug active compounds, spectrophotometry, validation, separation techniques, biological sample analysis, and modified electrodes. He has presented poster or oral presentation in national and international congresses and has ongoing studies. He received in 2018, ‘The Best Oral Presentation Award’ in 4th International Conference on Analytical Chemistry (RO-ICAC’2018).
